title: Cluster Headache
oneLiner: Severe unilateral headache with ipsilateral autonomic features (tearing, redness, rhinorrhoea), typically in young men; not fatal but needs urgent rule-out of secondary causes.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH):** thunderclap onset (peak within seconds), “worst headache of my life”, ± neck stiffness, photophobia, altered consciousness. Immediate CT head (non-contrast) if within 6 hours; if negative, LP for xanthochromia.
- **Bacterial meningitis:** headache + fever + neck stiffness + photophobia ± rash; Kernig’s sign. Start IV antibiotics (e.g., ceftriaxone 2g) and do LP if no contraindication *before* CT unless focal signs or decreased GCS.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Strictly unilateral orbital, supraorbital, temporal or frontal pain
  - Severe to excruciating intensity (rated 8-10/10)
  - Peaks within 5-10 minutes, lasts 15-180 minutes untreated
  - Clusters: attacks occur daily (1-8/day) for weeks to months, then remission (weeks to years)
  - Restlessness or agitation (pacing, rocking) – unlike migraine where patient lies still
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Ipsilateral conjunctival injection, lacrimation, rhinorrhoea/nasal congestion
  - Ipsilateral Horner’s (ptosis, miosis) – incomplete, resolves between attacks
  - May have forehead sweating, eyelid oedema
  - Neurological exam normal between attacks
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Migraine** – unilateral but usually pulsatile, photophobia/phonophobia, patient lies still; no severe autonomic features; longer duration (4-72 hours)
- **Paroxysmal hemicrania** – similar attack pattern but shorter (2-30 min), more frequent (>5/day), **absolutely responds to indomethacin** (trial: indomethacin 50 mg TDS)
- **SUNCT/SUNA** – stabbing/electrical pain around eye, brief (seconds-2 minutes), up to 200/day, prominent autonomic features
- **Secondary headache (must exclude):**
  - Carotid artery dissection: ipsilateral headache/neck pain + Horner’s (complete, persistent), ± transient ischaemic symptoms; go straight for CT angiography
  - Giant cell arteritis: age >50, raised ESR/CRP, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, visual symptoms; no clear cluster periodicity
  - Intracranial aneurysm: thunderclap onset; CT head + CTA
- **Splitter:** **High-flow oxygen (100% at 12-15 L/min) produces rapid relief within 15 minutes** in >70% of true cluster headache; fails in migraine and other TACs
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** HR, BP, temperature, GCS, fundoscopy (papilloedema? red reflex?), full neurological exam including pupils and extraocular movements
- **Bloods:** FBC, ESR, CRP (to screen for GCA/infection). LP if SAH suspected after negative CT (xanthochromia)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line for first presentation or atypical features:** urgent CT head (non-contrast) to exclude mass, SAH, hydrocephalus. If negative and suspicion of dissection/aneurysm: CT angiography/venography
  - **Second-line:** MRI brain with pituitary ± hypothalamic views (for structural causes) if attacks continue despite adequate trial of prophylaxis
- **Special tests:** Greater occipital nerve block (diagnostic and therapeutic in refractory cases)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** ICHD-3 (International Classification of Headache Disorders, 3rd edition) – at least 5 attacks fulfilling: (a) severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain lasting 15-180 min; (b) ipsilateral autonomic feature or restlessness; (c) frequency 1-8/day
- **Never-miss test:** **Urgent CT head (non-contrast) in any first-ever cluster headache** – especially if sudden onset, atypical features, or persistent Horner’s syndrome
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - High-flow oxygen 100% via non-rebreather mask at 12-15 L/min for 15-20 minutes (can repeat)
  - If oxygen fails or not tolerated: sumatriptan 6 mg SC (if no contraindications). Avoid sumatriptan if uncontrolled hypertension, ischaemic heart disease, or hemiplegic migraine.
  - For severe, prolonged attack (>3 hours): consider [check local policy] for IV dihydroergotamine (specialist use only)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - If admitted for status cluster headache (attack >3 hours or frequent attacks): IV dihydroergotamine 1 mg TDS (titrate; monitor BP, ECG). Alternative: high-dose prednisolone 60-80 mg PO for 2 weeks then taper (avoid >1 week without gastric protection). Consult neurology same day.
  - Start prophylactic therapy (see below) while still on acute treatment
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - First-line prophylactic: verapamil (start 40 mg TDS; titrate up to 240-480 mg daily as tolerated)
    - **Mandatory: ECG at baseline and after each dose increase** (risk of heart block, bradycardia)
  - Alternatives: topiramate (25 mg nocte → titrate to 100-150 mg daily), lithium (if failed verapamil; check renal function and levels), or greater occipital nerve block (injected by specialist)
  - Acute “bridge” therapy for cluster period: short course oral corticosteroids (prednisolone 60-80 mg/day for 1-2 weeks, then taper) until main prophylaxis takes effect
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% NaCl at 80 mL/h if NBM or dihydroergotamine use (prevent hypotension)
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH if high risk (e.g., prolonged immobility) – [check local policy]
  - Diet: as tolerated (oral fluids encouraged)
  - Observation frequency: 4-hourly HR, BP, GCS, NEWS2; continuous monitoring if on IV drugs affecting vitals
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line acute:**
  - Oxygen 100% 12-15 L/min via non-rebreather mask for 15-20 min – can repeat twice hourly
  - Sumatriptan 6 mg SC (max 12 mg/24h); rotate injection sites
  - Zolmitriptan 5 mg nasal spray (max 10 mg/24h) – alternative if SC not tolerated or available
- **First-line prophylactic:**
  - Verapamil SR 40 mg TDS initially; increase by 40-80 mg weekly to target 240-480 mg/day
  - ECG before each dose change – if PR >0.22 sec or any degree heart block, reduce dose or stop
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Topiramate 25 mg nocte, titrate by 25 mg weekly to 100-150 mg/day (watch for cognitive dulling, renal stones)
  - Prednisolone 60-80 mg daily for 1-2 weeks, then taper by 10 mg every 3 days – no longer than 3 weeks
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Triptans: avoid in ischaemic heart disease, uncontrolled hypertension, previous stroke, hemiplegic migraine; interact with MAOIs (washout 2 weeks)
  - Verapamil: avoid in heart block, sick sinus syndrome, severe hypotension; potent CYP3A4 inhibition (check interaction with simvastatin, ciclosporin)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Topiramate: reduce dose in moderate renal impairment (CrCl <70 mL/min) – halve dose; avoid if CrCl <30
  - Lithium: requires dose adjustment and monitoring (levels 0.6-1.0 mmol/L); avoid in acute kidney injury
  - Sumatriptan: no adjustment needed; use caution in hepatic impairment (first-pass metabolism)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Verapamil – starting high dose without ECG or titration** can cause complete heart block and cardiac arrest. Always start low and titrate with ECG.
  - **Sumatriptan SC – giving within 24 hours of ergotamine/dihydroergotamine** (risk of additive vasospasm). Use at least 6-hour washout.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Suicide ideation due to severity of pain (ask directly about suicidal thoughts)
  - Medication-overuse headache if using triptans >10 days/month
  - Hormonal side effects from high-dose steroids (hyperglycaemia, psychosis, infection)
- **Chronic:**
  - Chronic cluster headache (no remission within 1 year or remission <1 month) – difficult to treat
  - Prophylactic side effects: verapamil (constipation, bradycardia, hypotension); topiramate (paraesthesiae, cognitive slowing, weight loss); lithium (tremor, polyuria, hypothyroidism)
sections.Prognosis: - Not life-threatening but profoundly disabling during cluster periods. Episodic cluster (80%): attacks remit for months to years. Chronic cluster (20%): requires lifelong prophylaxis. With appropriate acute and prophylactic therapy, most patients achieve good symptom control.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always trial high-flow oxygen first** in the ED – it’s safe, effective, and can avoid triptan use. Explain the technique: non-rebreather mask, seal tight, 15 L/min for 15 min.
- **Check an ECG before starting verapamil** and after every dose increase – teach patients to report syncope or palpitations.
- **Consider greater occipital nerve block** as a rapid bridging therapy – can give immediate relief for 2-10 weeks and is safe for inpatients.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not diagnose cluster headache without neuroimaging** – especially if first attack or atypical features; missing secondary causes (aneurysm, dissection, mass) can be fatal.
- **Do not use opioids** – they are ineffective, cause medication overuse, and mask progression of secondary headache.
- **Do not give triptans without checking cardiovascular history** – a 34-year-old smoker on OCP with cluster headache could have undiagnosed coronary disease; ask about chest pain, claudication, stroke.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Sudden severe headache unlike previous attacks (thunderclap)
  - Fever, neck stiffness, photophobia, or rash
  - New focal neurological deficit or Horner’s that persists after attack resolves
  - Acute treatment (oxygen/triptan) does not abort attack within 30 minutes
- **Follow-up:** Neurology clinic (urgent, within 1-2 weeks if new diagnosis) or headache specialist. Provide headache diary to record attacks.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior or neurology on-call if:**
  - GCS <15 or new focal neurological signs (rule out stroke, mass)
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (tachycardia, hypotension, hypoxia)
  - Suspected SAH/meningitis – start immediate management and contact neurosurgery/infectious diseases
  - Patient with known cluster headache develops status cluster (>3 hours continuous) unresponsive to oxygen and triptans – may need IV dihydroergotamine under specialist supervision
- **Crash team:** call for any cardiac arrest, suspected severe bradycardia/heart block from verapamil (give IV calcium gluconate 10 mL of 10% and atropine if needed)

rawMarkdown:
## Cluster Headache
**One-liner:** Severe unilateral headache with ipsilateral autonomic features (tearing, redness, rhinorrhoea), typically in young men; not fatal but needs urgent rule-out of secondary causes.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH):** thunderclap onset (peak within seconds), “worst headache of my life”, ± neck stiffness, photophobia, altered consciousness. Immediate CT head (non-contrast) if within 6 hours; if negative, LP for xanthochromia.
- **Bacterial meningitis:** headache + fever + neck stiffness + photophobia ± rash; Kernig’s sign. Start IV antibiotics (e.g., ceftriaxone 2g) and do LP if no contraindication *before* CT unless focal signs or decreased GCS.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Strictly unilateral orbital, supraorbital, temporal or frontal pain
  - Severe to excruciating intensity (rated 8-10/10)
  - Peaks within 5-10 minutes, lasts 15-180 minutes untreated
  - Clusters: attacks occur daily (1-8/day) for weeks to months, then remission (weeks to years)
  - Restlessness or agitation (pacing, rocking) – unlike migraine where patient lies still
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Ipsilateral conjunctival injection, lacrimation, rhinorrhoea/nasal congestion
  - Ipsilateral Horner’s (ptosis, miosis) – incomplete, resolves between attacks
  - May have forehead sweating, eyelid oedema
  - Neurological exam normal between attacks

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Migraine** – unilateral but usually pulsatile, photophobia/phonophobia, patient lies still; no severe autonomic features; longer duration (4-72 hours)
- **Paroxysmal hemicrania** – similar attack pattern but shorter (2-30 min), more frequent (>5/day), **absolutely responds to indomethacin** (trial: indomethacin 50 mg TDS)
- **SUNCT/SUNA** – stabbing/electrical pain around eye, brief (seconds-2 minutes), up to 200/day, prominent autonomic features
- **Secondary headache (must exclude):**
  - Carotid artery dissection: ipsilateral headache/neck pain + Horner’s (complete, persistent), ± transient ischaemic symptoms; go straight for CT angiography
  - Giant cell arteritis: age >50, raised ESR/CRP, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, visual symptoms; no clear cluster periodicity
  - Intracranial aneurysm: thunderclap onset; CT head + CTA
- **Splitter:** **High-flow oxygen (100% at 12-15 L/min) produces rapid relief within 15 minutes** in >70% of true cluster headache; fails in migraine and other TACs

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** HR, BP, temperature, GCS, fundoscopy (papilloedema? red reflex?), full neurological exam including pupils and extraocular movements
- **Bloods:** FBC, ESR, CRP (to screen for GCA/infection). LP if SAH suspected after negative CT (xanthochromia)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line for first presentation or atypical features:** urgent CT head (non-contrast) to exclude mass, SAH, hydrocephalus. If negative and suspicion of dissection/aneurysm: CT angiography/venography
  - **Second-line:** MRI brain with pituitary ± hypothalamic views (for structural causes) if attacks continue despite adequate trial of prophylaxis
- **Special tests:** Greater occipital nerve block (diagnostic and therapeutic in refractory cases)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** ICHD-3 (International Classification of Headache Disorders, 3rd edition) – at least 5 attacks fulfilling: (a) severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain lasting 15-180 min; (b) ipsilateral autonomic feature or restlessness; (c) frequency 1-8/day
- **Never-miss test:** **Urgent CT head (non-contrast) in any first-ever cluster headache** – especially if sudden onset, atypical features, or persistent Horner’s syndrome

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - High-flow oxygen 100% via non-rebreather mask at 12-15 L/min for 15-20 minutes (can repeat)
  - If oxygen fails or not tolerated: sumatriptan 6 mg SC (if no contraindications). Avoid sumatriptan if uncontrolled hypertension, ischaemic heart disease, or hemiplegic migraine.
  - For severe, prolonged attack (>3 hours): consider [check local policy] for IV dihydroergotamine (specialist use only)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - If admitted for status cluster headache (attack >3 hours or frequent attacks): IV dihydroergotamine 1 mg TDS (titrate; monitor BP, ECG). Alternative: high-dose prednisolone 60-80 mg PO for 2 weeks then taper (avoid >1 week without gastric protection). Consult neurology same day.
  - Start prophylactic therapy (see below) while still on acute treatment
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - First-line prophylactic: verapamil (start 40 mg TDS; titrate up to 240-480 mg daily as tolerated)
    - **Mandatory: ECG at baseline and after each dose increase** (risk of heart block, bradycardia)
  - Alternatives: topiramate (25 mg nocte → titrate to 100-150 mg daily), lithium (if failed verapamil; check renal function and levels), or greater occipital nerve block (injected by specialist)
  - Acute “bridge” therapy for cluster period: short course oral corticosteroids (prednisolone 60-80 mg/day for 1-2 weeks, then taper) until main prophylaxis takes effect
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% NaCl at 80 mL/h if NBM or dihydroergotamine use (prevent hypotension)
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH if high risk (e.g., prolonged immobility) – [check local policy]
  - Diet: as tolerated (oral fluids encouraged)
  - Observation frequency: 4-hourly HR, BP, GCS, NEWS2; continuous monitoring if on IV drugs affecting vitals

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line acute:**
  - Oxygen 100% 12-15 L/min via non-rebreather mask for 15-20 min – can repeat twice hourly
  - Sumatriptan 6 mg SC (max 12 mg/24h); rotate injection sites
  - Zolmitriptan 5 mg nasal spray (max 10 mg/24h) – alternative if SC not tolerated or available
- **First-line prophylactic:**
  - Verapamil SR 40 mg TDS initially; increase by 40-80 mg weekly to target 240-480 mg/day
  - ECG before each dose change – if PR >0.22 sec or any degree heart block, reduce dose or stop
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Topiramate 25 mg nocte, titrate by 25 mg weekly to 100-150 mg/day (watch for cognitive dulling, renal stones)
  - Prednisolone 60-80 mg daily for 1-2 weeks, then taper by 10 mg every 3 days – no longer than 3 weeks
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Triptans: avoid in ischaemic heart disease, uncontrolled hypertension, previous stroke, hemiplegic migraine; interact with MAOIs (washout 2 weeks)
  - Verapamil: avoid in heart block, sick sinus syndrome, severe hypotension; potent CYP3A4 inhibition (check interaction with simvastatin, ciclosporin)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Topiramate: reduce dose in moderate renal impairment (CrCl <70 mL/min) – halve dose; avoid if CrCl <30
  - Lithium: requires dose adjustment and monitoring (levels 0.6-1.0 mmol/L); avoid in acute kidney injury
  - Sumatriptan: no adjustment needed; use caution in hepatic impairment (first-pass metabolism)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Verapamil – starting high dose without ECG or titration** can cause complete heart block and cardiac arrest. Always start low and titrate with ECG.
  - **Sumatriptan SC – giving within 24 hours of ergotamine/dihydroergotamine** (risk of additive vasospasm). Use at least 6-hour washout.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Suicide ideation due to severity of pain (ask directly about suicidal thoughts)
  - Medication-overuse headache if using triptans >10 days/month
  - Hormonal side effects from high-dose steroids (hyperglycaemia, psychosis, infection)
- **Chronic:**
  - Chronic cluster headache (no remission within 1 year or remission <1 month) – difficult to treat
  - Prophylactic side effects: verapamil (constipation, bradycardia, hypotension); topiramate (paraesthesiae, cognitive slowing, weight loss); lithium (tremor, polyuria, hypothyroidism)

### Prognosis
- Not life-threatening but profoundly disabling during cluster periods. Episodic cluster (80%): attacks remit for months to years. Chronic cluster (20%): requires lifelong prophylaxis. With appropriate acute and prophylactic therapy, most patients achieve good symptom control.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always trial high-flow oxygen first** in the ED – it’s safe, effective, and can avoid triptan use. Explain the technique: non-rebreather mask, seal tight, 15 L/min for 15 min.
- **Check an ECG before starting verapamil** and after every dose increase – teach patients to report syncope or palpitations.
- **Consider greater occipital nerve block** as a rapid bridging therapy – can give immediate relief for 2-10 weeks and is safe for inpatients.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not diagnose cluster headache without neuroimaging** – especially if first attack or atypical features; missing secondary causes (aneurysm, dissection, mass) can be fatal.
- **Do not use opioids** – they are ineffective, cause medication overuse, and mask progression of secondary headache.
- **Do not give triptans without checking cardiovascular history** – a 34-year-old smoker on OCP with cluster headache could have undiagnosed coronary disease; ask about chest pain, claudication, stroke.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Sudden severe headache unlike previous attacks (thunderclap)
  - Fever, neck stiffness, photophobia, or rash
  - New focal neurological deficit or Horner’s that persists after attack resolves
  - Acute treatment (oxygen/triptan) does not abort attack within 30 minutes
- **Follow-up:** Neurology clinic (urgent, within 1-2 weeks if new diagnosis) or headache specialist. Provide headache diary to record attacks.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior or neurology on-call if:**
  - GCS <15 or new focal neurological signs (rule out stroke, mass)
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (tachycardia, hypotension, hypoxia)
  - Suspected SAH/meningitis – start immediate management and contact neurosurgery/infectious diseases
  - Patient with known cluster headache develops status cluster (>3 hours continuous) unresponsive to oxygen and triptans – may need IV dihydroergotamine under specialist supervision
- **Crash team:** call for any cardiac arrest, suspected severe bradycardia/heart block from verapamil (give IV calcium gluconate 10 mL of 10% and atropine if needed)
